html:
Act I

Scene: Country public-house or shebeen, very rough and untidy. There is a sort of counter on the right with shelves, holding many bottles and jugs, just seen above it. Empty barrels stand near the counter. At back, a little to left of counter, there is a door into the open air, then, more to the left, there is a settle with shelves above it, with more jugs, and a table beneath a window. At the left there is a large open fireplace, with turf fire, and a small door into inner room. Pegeen, a wild-looking but fine girl, of about twenty, is writing at table. She is dressed in the usual peasant dress.

Pegeen Slowly as she writes. Six yards of stuff for to make a yellow gown. A pair of lace boots with lengthy heels on them and brassy eyes. A hat is suited for a wedding-day. A fine tooth comb. To be sent with three barrels of porter in Jimmy Farrell‚Äôs creel cart on the evening of the coming Fair to Mister Michael James Flaherty. With the best compliments of this season. Margaret Flaherty.
Shawn A fat and fair young man comes in as she signs, looks round awkwardly, when he sees she is alone. Where‚Äôs himself?
Pegeen Without looking at him. He‚Äôs coming. She directs the letter. To Mister Sheamus Mulroy, Wine and Spirit Dealer, Castlebar.
Shawn Uneasily. I didn‚Äôt see him on the road.
Pegeen How would you see him Licks stamp and puts it on letter. and it dark night this half hour gone by?
Shawn Turning towards the door again. I stood a while outside wondering would I have a right to pass on or to walk in and see you, Pegeen Mike, Comes to fire. and I could hear the cows breathing, and sighing in the stillness of the air, and not a step moving any place from this gate to the bridge.
Pegeen Putting letter in envelope. It‚Äôs above at the crossroads he is, meeting Philly Cullen; and a couple more are going along with him to Kate Cassidy‚Äôs wake.
Shawn Looking at her blankly. And he‚Äôs going that length in the dark night?
Pegeen Impatiently. He is surely, and leaving me lonesome on the scruff of the hill. She gets up and puts envelope on dresser, then winds the clock. Isn‚Äôt it long the nights are now, Shawn Keogh, to be leaving a poor girl with her own self counting the hours to the dawn of day?
Shawn With awkward humour. If it is, when we‚Äôre wedded in a short while you‚Äôll have no call to complain, for I‚Äôve little will to be walking off to wakes or weddings in the darkness of the night.
Pegeen With rather scornful good humour. You‚Äôre making mighty certain, Shaneen, that I‚Äôll wed you now.
Shawn Aren‚Äôt we after making a good bargain, the way we‚Äôre only waiting these days on Father Reilly‚Äôs dispensation from the bishops, or the Court of Rome.
Pegeen Looking at him teasingly, washing up at dresser. It‚Äôs a wonder, Shaneen, the Holy Father‚Äôd be taking notice of the likes of you; for if I was him I wouldn‚Äôt bother with this place where you‚Äôll meet none but Red Linahan, has a squint in his eye, and Patcheen is lame in his heel, or the mad Mulrannies were driven from California and they lost in their wits. We‚Äôre a queer lot these times to go troubling the Holy Father on his sacred seat.
Shawn Scandalized. If we are, we‚Äôre as good this place as another, maybe, and as good these times as we were forever.
Pegeen With scorn. As good, is it? Where now will you meet the like of Daneen Sullivan knocked the eye from a peeler, or Marcus Quin, God rest him, got six months for maiming ewes, and he a great warrant to tell stories of holy Ireland till he‚Äôd have the old women shedding down tears about their feet. Where will you find the like of them, I‚Äôm saying?
Shawn Timidly. If you don‚Äôt it‚Äôs a good job, maybe; for With peculiar emphasis on the words. Father Reilly has small conceit to have that kind walking around and talking to the girls.
Pegeen Impatiently, throwing water from basin out of the door. Stop tormenting me with Father Reilly Imitating his voice. when I‚Äôm asking only what way I‚Äôll pass these twelve hours of dark, and not take my death with the fear. Looking out of door.
Shawn Timidly. Would I fetch you the Widow Quin, maybe?
Pegeen Is it the like of that murderer? You‚Äôll not, surely.
Shawn Going to her, soothingly. Then I‚Äôm thinking himself will stop along with you when he sees you taking on, for it‚Äôll be a long nighttime with great darkness, and I‚Äôm after feeling a kind of fellow above in the furzy ditch, groaning wicked like a maddening dog, the way it‚Äôs good cause you have, maybe, to be fearing now.
Pegeen Turning on him sharply. What‚Äôs that? Is it a man you seen?
Shawn Retreating. I couldn‚Äôt see him at all; but I heard him groaning out, and breaking his heart. It should have been a young man from his words speaking.
Pegeen Going after him. And you never went near to see was he hurted or what ailed him at all?
Shawn I did not, Pegeen Mike. It was a dark, lonesome place to be hearing the like of him.
Pegeen Well, you‚Äôre a daring fellow, and if they find his corpse stretched above in the dews of dawn, what‚Äôll you say then to the peelers, or the Justice of the Peace?
Shawn Thunderstruck. I wasn‚Äôt thinking of that. For the love of God, Pegeen Mike, don‚Äôt let on I was speaking of him. Don‚Äôt tell your father and the men is coming above; for if they heard that story, they‚Äôd have great blabbing this night at the wake.
Pegeen I‚Äôll maybe tell them, and I‚Äôll maybe not.
Shawn They are coming at the door. Will you whisht, I‚Äôm saying?
Pegeen Whisht yourself.
She goes behind counter. Michael James, fat jovial publican, comes in followed by Philly Cullen, who is thin and mistrusting, and Jimmy Farrell, who is fat and amorous, about forty-five.
Men Together. God bless you. The blessing of God on this place.
Pegeen God bless you kindly.
Michael To men who go to the counter. Sit down now, and take your rest. Crosses to Shawn at the fire. And how is it you are, Shawn Keogh? Are you coming over the sands to Kate Cassidy‚Äôs wake?
Shawn I am not, Michael James. I‚Äôm going home the shortcut to my bed.
Pegeen Speaking across the counter. He‚Äôs right too, and have you no shame, Michael James, to be quitting off for the whole night, and leaving myself lonesome in the shop?
Michael Good-humouredly. Isn‚Äôt it the same whether I go for the whole night or a part only? and I‚Äôm thinking it‚Äôs a queer daughter you are if you‚Äôd have me crossing backward through the Stooks of the Dead Women, with a drop taken.
Pegeen If I am a queer daughter, it‚Äôs a queer father‚Äôd be leaving me lonesome these twelve hours of dark, and I piling the turf with the dogs barking, and the calves mooing, and my own teeth rattling with the fear.
Jimmy Flatteringly. What is there to hurt you, and you a fine, hardy girl would knock the head of any two men in the place?
Pegeen Working herself up. Isn‚Äôt there the harvest boys with their tongues red for drink, and the ten tinkers is camped in the east glen, and the thousand militia‚ÅÝ‚Äîbad cess to them!‚ÅÝ‚Äîwalking idle through the land. There‚Äôs lots surely to hurt me, and I won‚Äôt stop alone in it, let himself do what he will.
Michael If you‚Äôre that afeard, let Shawn Keogh stop along with you. It‚Äôs the will of God, I‚Äôm thinking, himself should be seeing to you now. They all turn on Shawn.
Shawn In horrified confusion. I would and welcome, Michael James, but I‚Äôm afeard of Father Reilly; and what at all would the Holy Father and the Cardinals of Rome be saying if they heard I did the like of that?
Michael With contempt. God help you! Can‚Äôt you sit in by the hearth with the light lit and herself beyond in the room? You‚Äôll do that surely, for I‚Äôve heard tell there‚Äôs a queer fellow above, going mad or getting his death, maybe, in the grip of the ditch, so she‚Äôd be safer this night with a person here.
Shawn With plaintive despair. I‚Äôm afeard of Father Reilly, I‚Äôm saying. Let you not be tempting me, and we near married itself.
Philly With cold contempt. Lock him in the west room. He‚Äôll stay then and have no sin to be telling to the priest.
Michael To Shawn, getting between him and the door. Go up now.
Shawn At the top of his voice. Don‚Äôt stop me, Michael James. Let me out of the door, I‚Äôm saying, for the love of the Almighty God. Let me out Trying to dodge past him. Let me out of it, and may God grant you His indulgence in the hour of need.
Michael Loudly. Stop your noising, and sit down by the hearth. Gives him a push and goes to counter laughing.
Shawn Turning back, wringing his hands. Oh, Father Reilly and the saints of God, where will I hide myself today? Oh, St.¬ÝJoseph and St.¬ÝPatrick and St.¬ÝBrigid, and St.¬ÝJames, have mercy on me now! Shawn turns round, sees door clear, and makes a rush for it.
Michael Catching him by the coattail. You‚Äôd be going, is it?
Shawn Screaming. Leave me go, Michael James, leave me go, you old Pagan, leave me go, or I‚Äôll get the curse of the priests on you, and of the scarlet-coated bishops of the courts of Rome. With a sudden movement he pulls himself out of his coat, and disappears out of the door, leaving his coat in Michael‚Äôs hands.
Michael Turning round, and holding up coat. Well, there‚Äôs the coat of a Christian man. Oh, there‚Äôs sainted glory this day in the lonesome west; and by the will of God I‚Äôve got you a decent man, Pegeen, you‚Äôll have no call to be spying after if you‚Äôve a score of young girls, maybe, weeding in your fields.
Pegeen Taking up the defence of her property. What right have you to be making game of a poor fellow for minding the priest, when it‚Äôs your own the fault is, not paying a penny potboy to stand along with me and give me courage in the doing of my work? She snaps the coat away from him, and goes behind counter with it.
Michael Taken aback. Where would I get a potboy? Would you have me send the bellman screaming in the streets of Castlebar?
Shawn Opening the door a chink and putting in his head, in a small voice. Michael James!
Michael Imitating him. What ails you?
Shawn The queer dying fellow‚Äôs beyond looking over the ditch. He‚Äôs come up, I‚Äôm thinking, stealing your hens. Looks over his shoulder. God help me, he‚Äôs following me now, He runs into room. and if he‚Äôs heard what I said, he‚Äôll be having my life, and I going home lonesome in the darkness of the night.
For a perceptible moment they watch the door with curiosity. Someone coughs outside. Then Christy Mahon, a slight young man, comes in very tired and frightened and dirty.
Christy In a small voice. God save all here!
Men God save you kindly.
Christy Going to the counter. I‚Äôd trouble you for a glass of porter, woman of the house. He puts down coin.
Pegeen Serving him. You‚Äôre one of the tinkers, young fellow, is beyond camped in the glen?
Christy I am not; but I‚Äôm destroyed walking.
Michael Patronizingly. Let you come up then to the fire. You‚Äôre looking famished with the cold.
Christy God reward you. He takes up his glass and goes a little way across to the left, then stops and looks about him. Is it often the police do be coming into this place, master of the house?
Michael If you‚Äôd come in better hours, you‚Äôd have seen ‚ÄúLicensed for the sale of Beer and Spirits, to be consumed on the premises,‚Äù written in white letters above the door, and what would the polis want spying on me, and not a decent house within four miles, the way every living Christian is a bona fide, saving one widow alone?
Christy With relief. It‚Äôs a safe house, so. He goes over to the fire, sighing and moaning. Then he sits down, putting his glass beside him and begins gnawing a turnip, too miserable to feel the others staring at him with curiosity.
Michael Going after him. Is it yourself fearing the polis? You‚Äôre wanting, maybe?
Christy There‚Äôs many wanting.
Michael Many surely, with the broken harvest and the ended wars. He picks up some stockings, etc., that are near the fire, and carries them away furtively. It should be larceny, I‚Äôm thinking?
Christy Dolefully. I had it in my mind it was a different word and a bigger.
Pegeen There‚Äôs a queer lad. Were you never slapped in school, young fellow, that you don‚Äôt know the name of your deed?
Christy Bashfully. I‚Äôm slow at learning, a middling scholar only.
Michael If you‚Äôre a dunce itself, you‚Äôd have a right to know that larceny‚Äôs robbing and stealing. Is it for the like of that you‚Äôre wanting?
Christy With a flash of family pride. And I the son of a strong farmer, With a sudden qualm. God rest his soul, could have bought up the whole of your old house a while since, from the butt of his tailpocket, and not have missed the weight of it gone.
Michael Impressed. If it‚Äôs not stealing, it‚Äôs maybe something big.
Christy Flattered. Aye; it‚Äôs maybe something big.
Jimmy He‚Äôs a wicked-looking young fellow. Maybe he followed after a young woman on a lonesome night.
Christy Shocked. Oh, the saints forbid, mister; I was all times a decent lad.
Philly Turning on Jimmy. You‚Äôre a silly man, Jimmy Farrell. He said his father was a farmer a while since, and there‚Äôs himself now in a poor state. Maybe the land was grabbed from him, and he did what any decent man would do.
Michael To Christy, mysteriously. Was it bailiffs?
Christy The divil a one.
Michael Agents?
Christy The divil a one.
Michael Landlords?
Christy Peevishly. Ah, not at all, I‚Äôm saying. You‚Äôd see the like of them stories on any little paper of a Munster town. But I‚Äôm not calling to mind any person, gentle, simple, judge or jury, did the like of me.
They all draw nearer with delighted curiosity.
Philly Well, that lad‚Äôs a puzzle‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe world.
Jimmy He‚Äôd beat Dan Davies‚Äô circus, or the holy missioners making sermons on the villainy of man. Try him again, Philly.
Philly Did you strike golden guineas out of solder, young fellow, or shilling coins itself?
Christy I did not, mister, not sixpence nor a farthing coin.
Jimmy Did you marry three wives maybe? I‚Äôm told there‚Äôs a sprinkling have done that among the holy Luthers of the preaching north.
Christy Shyly. I never married with one, let alone with a couple or three.
Philly Maybe he went fighting for the Boers, the like of the man beyond, was judged to be hanged, quartered and drawn. Were you off east, young fellow, fighting bloody wars for Kruger and the freedom of the Boers?
Christy I never left my own parish till Tuesday was a week.
Pegeen Coming from counter. He‚Äôs done nothing, so. To Christy. If you didn‚Äôt commit murder or a bad, nasty thing, or false coining, or robbery, or butchery, or the like of them, there isn‚Äôt anything that would be worth your troubling for to run from now. You did nothing at all.
Christy His feelings hurt. That‚Äôs an unkindly thing to be saying to a poor orphaned traveller, has a prison behind him, and hanging before, and hell‚Äôs gap gaping below.
Pegeen With a sign to the men to be quiet. You‚Äôre only saying it. You did nothing at all. A soft lad the like of you wouldn‚Äôt slit the windpipe of a screeching sow.
Christy Offended. You‚Äôre not speaking the truth.
Pegeen In mock rage. Not speaking the truth, is it? Would you have me knock the head of you with the butt of the broom?
Christy Twisting round on her with a sharp cry of horror. Don‚Äôt strike me. I killed my poor father, Tuesday was a week, for doing the like of that.
Pegeen With blank amazement. Is it killed your father?
Christy Subsiding. With the help of God I did surely, and that the Holy Immaculate Mother may intercede for his soul.
Philly Retreating with Jimmy. There‚Äôs a daring fellow.
Jimmy Oh, glory be to God!
Michael With great respect. That was a hanging crime, mister honey. You should have had good reason for doing the like of that.
Christy In a very reasonable tone. He was a dirty man, God forgive him, and he getting old and crusty, the way I couldn‚Äôt put up with him at all.
Pegeen And you shot him dead?
Christy Shaking his head. I never used weapons. I‚Äôve no license, and I‚Äôm a law-fearing man.
Michael It was with a hilted knife maybe? I‚Äôm told, in the big world it‚Äôs bloody knives they use.
Christy Loudly, scandalized. Do you take me for a slaughter-boy?
Pegeen You never hanged him, the way Jimmy Farrell hanged his dog from the license, and had it screeching and wriggling three hours at the butt of a string, and himself swearing it was a dead dog, and the peelers swearing it had life?
Christy I did not then. I just riz the loy and let fall the edge of it on the ridge of his skull, and he went down at my feet like an empty sack, and never let a grunt or groan from him at all.
Michael Making a sign to Pegeen to fill Christy‚Äôs glass. And what way weren‚Äôt you hanged, mister? Did you bury him then?
Christy Considering. Aye. I buried him then. Wasn‚Äôt I digging spuds in the field?
Michael And the peelers never followed after you the eleven days that you‚Äôre out?
Christy Shaking his head. Never a one of them, and I walking forward facing hog, dog, or divil on the highway of the road.
Philly Nodding wisely. It‚Äôs only with a common weekday kind of a murderer them lads would be trusting their carcase, and that man should be a great terror when his temper‚Äôs roused.
Michael He should then. To Christy. And where was it, mister honey, that you did the deed?
Christy Looking at him with suspicion. Oh, a distant place, master of the house, a windy corner of high, distant hills.
Philly Nodding with approval. He‚Äôs a close man, and he‚Äôs right, surely.
Pegeen That‚Äôd be a lad with the sense of Solomon to have for a potboy, Michael James, if it‚Äôs the truth you‚Äôre seeking one at all.
Philly The peelers is fearing him, and if you‚Äôd that lad in the house there isn‚Äôt one of them would come smelling around if the dogs itself were lapping poteen from the dungpit of the yard.
Jimmy Bravery‚Äôs a treasure in a lonesome place, and a lad would kill his father, I‚Äôm thinking, would face a foxy divil with a pitchpike on the flags of hell.
Pegeen It‚Äôs the truth they‚Äôre saying, and if I‚Äôd that lad in the house, I wouldn‚Äôt be fearing the loosed kharki cutthroats, or the walking dead.
Christy Swelling with surprise and triumph. Well, glory be to God!
Michael With deference. Would you think well to stop here and be potboy, mister honey, if we gave you good wages, and didn‚Äôt destroy you with the weight of work?
Shawn Coming forward uneasily. That‚Äôd be a queer kind to bring into a decent quiet household with the like of Pegeen Mike.
Pegeen Very sharply. Will you whisht? Who‚Äôs speaking to you?
Shawn Retreating. A bloody-handed murderer the like of.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶
Pegeen Snapping at him. Whisht I am saying; we‚Äôll take no fooling from your like at all. To Christy with a honeyed voice. And you, young fellow, you‚Äôd have a right to stop, I‚Äôm thinking, for we‚Äôd do our all and utmost to content your needs.
Christy Overcome with wonder. And I‚Äôd be safe in this place from the searching law?
Michael You would, surely. If they‚Äôre not fearing you, itself, the peelers in this place is decent droughty poor fellows, wouldn‚Äôt touch a cur dog and not give warning in the dead of night.
Pegeen Very kindly and persuasively. Let you stop a short while anyhow. Aren‚Äôt you destroyed walking with your feet in bleeding blisters, and your whole skin needing washing like a Wicklow sheep.
Christy Looking round with satisfaction. It‚Äôs a nice room, and if it‚Äôs not humbugging me you are, I‚Äôm thinking that I‚Äôll surely stay.
Jimmy Jumps up. Now, by the grace of God, herself will be safe this night, with a man killed his father holding danger from the door, and let you come on, Michael James, or they‚Äôll have the best stuff drunk at the wake.
Michael Going to the door with men. And begging your pardon, mister, what name will we call you, for we‚Äôd like to know?
Christy Christopher Mahon.
Michael Well, God bless you, Christy, and a good rest till we meet again when the sun‚Äôll be rising to the noon of day.
Christy God bless you all.
Men God bless you. They go out except Shawn, who lingers at door.
Shawn To Pegeen. Are you wanting me to stop along with you and keep you from harm?
Pegeen Gruffly. Didn‚Äôt you say you were fearing Father Reilly?
Shawn There‚Äôd be no harm staying now, I‚Äôm thinking, and himself in it too.
Pegeen You wouldn‚Äôt stay when there was need for you, and let you step off nimble this time when there‚Äôs none.
Shawn Didn‚Äôt I say it was Father Reilly.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶
Pegeen Go on, then, to Father Reilly, In a jeering tone. and let him put you in the holy brotherhoods, and leave that lad to me.
Shawn If I meet the Widow Quin.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶
Pegeen Go on, I‚Äôm saying, and don‚Äôt be waking this place with your noise. She hustles him out and bolts the door. That lad would wear the spirits from the saints of peace. Bustles about, then takes off her apron and pins it up in the window as a blind. Christy watching her timidly. Then she comes to him and speaks with bland good-humour. Let you stretch out now by the fire, young fellow. You should be destroyed travelling.
Christy Shyly again, drawing off his boots. I‚Äôm tired, surely, walking wild eleven days, and waking fearful in the night. He holds up one of his feet, feeling his blisters, and looking at them with compassion.
Pegeen Standing beside him, watching him with delight. You should have had great people in your family, I‚Äôm thinking, with the little, small feet you have, and you with a kind of a quality name, the like of what you‚Äôd find on the great powers and potentates of France and Spain.
Christy With pride. We were great surely, with wide and windy acres of rich Munster land.
Pegeen Wasn‚Äôt I telling you, and you a fine, handsome young fellow with a noble brow?
Christy With a flash of delighted surprise. Is it me?
Pegeen Aye. Did you never hear that from the young girls where you come from in the west or south?
Christy With venom. I did not then. Oh, they‚Äôre bloody liars in the naked parish where I grew a man.
Pegeen If they are itself, you‚Äôve heard it these days, I‚Äôm thinking, and you walking the world telling out your story to young girls or old.
Christy I‚Äôve told my story no place till this night, Pegeen Mike, and it‚Äôs foolish I was here, maybe, to be talking free, but you‚Äôre decent people, I‚Äôm thinking, and yourself a kindly woman, the way I wasn‚Äôt fearing you at all.
Pegeen Filling a sack with straw. You‚Äôve said the like of that, maybe, in every cot and cabin where you‚Äôve met a young girl on your way.
Christy Going over to her, gradually raising his voice. I‚Äôve said it nowhere till this night, I‚Äôm telling you, for I‚Äôve seen none the like of you the eleven long days I am walking the world, looking over a low ditch or a high ditch on my north or my south, into stony scattered fields, or scribes of bog, where you‚Äôd see young, limber girls, and fine prancing women making laughter with the men.
Pegeen If you weren‚Äôt destroyed travelling, you‚Äôd have as much talk and streeleen, I‚Äôm thinking, as Owen Roe O‚ÄôSullivan or the poets of the Dingle Bay, and I‚Äôve heard all times it‚Äôs the poets are your like, fine fiery fellows with great rages when their temper‚Äôs roused.
Christy Drawing a little nearer to her. You‚Äôve a power of rings, God bless you, and would there be any offence if I was asking are you single now?
Pegeen What would I want wedding so young?
Christy With relief. We‚Äôre alike, so.
Pegeen She puts sack on settle and beats it up. I never killed my father. I‚Äôd be afeard to do that, except I was the like of yourself with blind rages tearing me within, for I‚Äôm thinking you should have had great tussling when the end was come.
Christy Expanding with delight at the first confidential talk he has ever had with a woman. We had not then. It was a hard woman was come over the hill, and if he was always a crusty kind when he‚Äôd a hard woman setting him on, not the divil himself or his four fathers could put up with him at all.
Pegeen With curiosity. And isn‚Äôt it a great wonder that one wasn‚Äôt fearing you?
Christy Very confidentially. Up to the day I killed my father, there wasn‚Äôt a person in Ireland knew the kind I was, and I there drinking, waking, eating, sleeping, a quiet, simple poor fellow with no man giving me heed.
Pegeen Getting a quilt out of the cupboard and putting it on the sack. It was the girls were giving you heed maybe, and I‚Äôm thinking it‚Äôs most conceit you‚Äôd have to be gaming with their like.
Christy Shaking his head, with simplicity. Not the girls itself, and I won‚Äôt tell you a lie. There wasn‚Äôt anyone heeding me in that place saving only the dumb beasts of the field. He sits down at fire.
Pegeen With disappointment. And I thinking you should have been living the like of a king of Norway or the Eastern world. She comes and sits beside him after placing bread and mug of milk on the table.
Christy Laughing piteously. The like of a king, is it? And I after toiling, moiling, digging, dodging from the dawn till dusk with never a sight of joy or sport saving only when I‚Äôd be abroad in the dark night poaching rabbits on hills, for I was a divil to poach, God forgive me, Very naively. and I near got six months for going with a dung fork and stabbing a fish.
Pegeen And it‚Äôs that you‚Äôd call sport, is it, to be abroad in the darkness with yourself alone?
Christy I did, God help me, and there I‚Äôd be as happy as the sunshine of St.¬ÝMartin‚Äôs Day, watching the light passing the north or the patches of fog, till I‚Äôd hear a rabbit starting to screech and I‚Äôd go running in the furze. Then when I‚Äôd my full share I‚Äôd come walking down where you‚Äôd see the ducks and geese stretched sleeping on the highway of the road, and before I‚Äôd pass the dunghill, I‚Äôd hear himself snoring out, a loud lonesome snore he‚Äôd be making all times, the while he was sleeping, and he a man ‚Äôd be raging all times, the while he was waking, like a gaudy officer you‚Äôd hear cursing and damning and swearing oaths.
Pegeen Providence and Mercy, spare us all!
Christy It‚Äôs that you‚Äôd say surely if you seen him and he after drinking for weeks, rising up in the red dawn, or before it maybe, and going out into the yard as naked as an ash tree in the moon of May, and shying clods against the visage of the stars till he‚Äôd put the fear of death into the banbhs and the screeching sows.
Pegeen I‚Äôd be well-nigh afeard of that lad myself, I‚Äôm thinking. And there was no one in it but the two of you alone?
Christy The divil a one, though he‚Äôd sons and daughters walking all great states and territories of the world, and not a one of them, to this day, but would say their seven curses on him, and they rousing up to let a cough or sneeze, maybe, in the deadness of the night.
Pegeen Nodding her head. Well, you should have been a queer lot. I never cursed my father the like of that, though I‚Äôm twenty and more years of age.
Christy Then you‚Äôd have cursed mine, I‚Äôm telling you, and he a man never gave peace to any, saving when he‚Äôd get two months or three, or be locked in the asylums for battering peelers or assaulting men With depression. the way it was a bitter life he led me till I did up a Tuesday and halve his skull.
Pegeen Putting her hand on his shoulder. Well, you‚Äôll have peace in this place, Christy Mahon, and none to trouble you, and it‚Äôs near time a fine lad like you should have your good share of the earth.

Someone knocks.
Christy Clinging to Pegeen. Oh, glory! it‚Äôs late for knocking, and this last while I‚Äôm in terror of the peelers, and the walking dead.

Pegeen Who‚Äôs there?
Voice Outside. Me.
Pegeen Who‚Äôs me?
Voice The Widow Quin.
Pegeen Jumping up and giving him the bread and milk. Go on now with your supper, and let on to be sleepy, for if she found you were such a warrant to talk, she‚Äôd be stringing gabble till the dawn of day.
He takes bread and sits shyly with his back to the door.
Pegeen Opening door, with temper. What ails you, or what is it you‚Äôre wanting at this hour of the night?
Widow Quin Coming in a step and peering at Christy. I‚Äôm after meeting Shawn Keogh and Father Reilly below, who told me of your curiosity man, and they fearing by this time he was maybe roaring, romping on your hands with drink.
Pegeen Pointing to Christy. Look now is he roaring, and he stretched away drowsy with his supper and his mug of milk. Walk down and tell that to Father Reilly and to Shaneen Keogh.
Widow Quin Coming forward. I‚Äôll not see them again, for I‚Äôve their word to lead that lad forward for to lodge with me.

Widow Quin Going over. This night. ‚ÄúIt isn‚Äôt fitting,‚Äù says the priesteen, ‚Äúto have his likeness lodging with an orphaned girl.‚Äù To Christy. God save you, mister!

Widow Quin Looking at him with half-amazed curiosity. Well, aren‚Äôt you a little smiling fellow? It should have been great and bitter torments did rouse your spirits to a deed of blood.
Christy Doubtfully. It should, maybe.
Widow Quin It‚Äôs more than ‚Äúmaybe‚Äù I‚Äôm saying, and it‚Äôd soften my heart to see you sitting so simple with your cup and cake, and you fitter to be saying your catechism than slaying your da.
Pegeen At counter, washing glasses. There‚Äôs talking when any‚Äôd see he‚Äôs fit to be holding his head high with the wonders of the world. Walk on from this, for I‚Äôll not have him tormented and he destroyed travelling since Tuesday was a week.

Pegeen Contemptuously. She did not. She hit himself with a worn pick, and the rusted poison did corrode his blood the way he never overed it, and died after. That was a sneaky kind of murder did win small glory with the boys itself. She crosses to Christy‚Äôs left.
Widow Quin With good-humour. If it didn‚Äôt, maybe all knows a widow woman has buried her children and destroyed her man is a wiser comrade for a young lad than a girl, the like of you, who‚Äôd go helter-skeltering after any man would let you a wink upon the road.
Pegeen Breaking out into wild rage. And you‚Äôll say that, Widow Quin, and you gasping with the rage you had racing the hill beyond to look on his face.
Widow Quin Laughing derisively. Me, is it? Well, Father Reilly has cuteness to divide you now. She pulls Christy up. There‚Äôs great temptation in a man did slay his da, and we‚Äôd best be going, young fellow; so rise up and come with me.

Widow Quin It‚Äôd be a crazy potboy‚Äôd lodge him in the shebeen where he works by day, so you‚Äôd have a right to come on, young fellow, till you see my little houseen, a perch off on the rising hill.

Doesn‚Äôt the world know you reared a black lamb at your own breast, so that the Lord Bishop of Connaught felt the elements of a Christian, and he eating it after in a kidney stew? Widow Quin With amusement. Do you hear her now, young fellow? Do you hear the way she‚Äôll be rating at your own self when a week is by?
Pegeen To Christy. Don‚Äôt heed her. Tell her to go into her pigsty and not plague us here.
Widow Quin I‚Äôm going; but he‚Äôll come with me.
Pegeen Shaking him. Are you dumb, young fellow?
Christy Timidly, to Widow Quin. God increase you; but I‚Äôm potboy in this place, and it‚Äôs here I‚Äôd liefer stay.
Pegeen Triumphantly. Now you have heard him, and go on from this.
Widow Quin Looking round the room. It‚Äôs lonesome this hour crossing the hill, and if he won‚Äôt come along with me, I‚Äôd have a right maybe to stop this night with yourselves. Let me stretch out on the settle, Pegeen Mike; and himself can lie by the hearth.

God bless you now, young fellow, and let you be wary, or there‚Äôs right torment will await you here if you go romancing with her like, and she waiting only, as they bade me say, on a sheepskin parchment to be wed with Shawn Keogh of Killakeen.

Christy As she goes to inner room. May God and Mary and St.¬ÝPatrick bless you and reward you, for your kindly talk. She shuts the door behind her.
